full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Recently, on a visit with my parents, my mother reminded me I attended around 80 funerals<|7.54|> <|7.54|> before the age of 18.<|10.38|> <|10.38|> And this is not because I was a particularly devout minor, but because my maternal grandmother<|16.54|> <|16.54|> was the minister of music in our church, and my mother believes in funerals.<|23.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is very difficult to describe my childhood without discussing death and funerals.<|7.76|> <|7.76|> When my parents would get word of a community member or a loved one's death, the first question<|13.16|> <|13.16|> they'd ask is, "Who's hosting?"<|17.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> which was shorthand for whose house are we supposed to take food and drinks and hugs and loves to?<|9.60|> <|9.98|> Where are we keeping vigil until the funeral?<|13.20|> <|14.24|> And we'd pick up a box of fried chicken and a 12-pack of soda and head to the deceased family member's home.<|20.90|> <|21.20|> We didn't take wine. We were Baptist.<|23.28|> <|24.06|> And my parents would show up with food and drinks and hugs and...<|28.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and the process of mourning would begin.<|2.80|> <|4.00|> If this was day one of the funeral octave,<|7.60|> <|7.60|> there could be between five to ten days more of visiting the grieving family,<|13.04|> <|13.04|> running errands for them, pitching in for funeral costs,<|17.20|> <|17.20|> and landing back at the designated house immediately after the funeral.<|22.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This was the first 17 years of my life, and sometimes I wonder how I ended up a priest.<|8.76|> <|11.76|> Because of the nature of my grandmother's position in the church and my parents' lifetime of relationships in the city of their respective births,<|20.42|> <|20.42|> I was wrapped in an ecosystem of undertakers—<|25.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Funeral directors and pastors and what we would call professional mourners that I assumed every child had.<|10.48|> <|11.34|> There was in this cast of characters a flamboyant, legendary funeral director who<|18.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> who buried many of the people we knew and conducted a large share of black funerals in my hometown,<|6.74|> <|6.74|> much to the chagrin of more established funeral homes.<|11.48|> <|12.26|> When someone on my grandmother's street would die, Dr. Spencer, the funeral director,<|18.86|> <|19.52|> would stop by my grandmother's house and say,<|22.58|> <|23.12|> "'Now, Sister Turner, when I die, I want you to sing at my funeral.'"<|28.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> "But I want you to sing at my funeral," my grandmother would tell Dr. Spencer.<|6.00|> <|6.00|> Dr. Spencer ended up dying tragically at a young age, 10 years before my own grandmother's death.<|15.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For whatever reason, I was raised with a healthy appreciation for death.<|4.98|> <|5.52|> Not just through exposure to the rituals of my Native community,<|9.58|> <|9.58|> but through regular discussions about death with my parents and grandparents<|14.58|> <|14.58|> who were mindful of the ways that they spoke about death.<|18.66|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And how could they not be?<|3.28|> <|3.28|> That same funeral director who joked with my grandmother about singing at his funeral<|8.22|> <|8.22|> would ask my mother after funerals, "Are you in your expedition, Sister Greer?"<|14.76|> <|14.76|> "Yes," she would say.<|17.18|> <|17.18|> "Well, I need you to transport these flowers to the cemetery for me."<|22.50|> <|22.50|> And away we would go to the cemetery with funeral flowers in the trunk of my mom's SUV.<|28.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The flurry of activity around funerals I experienced in childhood is not unlike the many hands that make burials here at St. John's Cathedral possible.<|13.94|> <|13.94|> From the flower team ably arranging flowers,<|20.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> to vergers and acolytes willingly offering their time<|4.30|> <|4.30|> to literally walk with families<|6.76|> <|6.76|> through the worst day of their lives,<|8.58|> <|8.80|> to lay pastoral visitors who offer their ears<|12.28|> <|12.28|> to listen to hospice patients,<|14.72|> <|14.94|> to our facility staff members<|17.40|> <|17.40|> who open the crips on All Souls Walk<|20.22|> <|20.22|> so that the remains of those we bury<|22.34|> <|22.34|> will have a neat, safe, final resting place,<|26.40|> <|26.90|> the wake of death,<|28.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> is a communal effort. Because in our tradition, we believe that no one dies<|7.56|> <|7.56|> alone, that no one is buried alone. We are each other's witnesses, the ones who<|16.14|> <|16.14|> say by our presence, "I saw you in life and I see you in death."<|23.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Gregory Hillis, a scholar of the 20th century monk Thomas Merton, recently attended a burial<|9.84|> <|9.84|> of a monk, Brother Harold, at the Abbey of Gethsemane, where Merton lived and worked<|17.20|> <|17.20|> and died.<|18.06|> <|18.06|> And,<|18.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In a short essay in Common Will magazine, Hillis parses out the most gentle moments of Brother Harold's burial.<|9.74|> <|11.36|> Quote,<|11.86|> <|13.14|> Along with the monks and members of Brother Harold's family, I processed to a freshly dug grave,<|20.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Cistercians dig their graves very deep and they bury their dead without caskets.<|7.76|> <|7.76|> From my perch, I could see that a pillow had been placed in the grave on which had been placed a flower.<|15.84|> <|15.84|> There was also a ladder leading into the grave.<|19.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As his brothers lowered Brother Harold down, the monk standing in the grave gingerly held Brother Harold's head the way my wife and I would put each of our newborn sons into the crib, doing all we could to make sure that his sleep wasn't disturbed.<|19.26|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Hillis ends his essay by saying, "I witnessed the care and love of a community for one of<|7.64|> <|7.64|> their own, a care that extended to the very depths of the grave and learned that to confront<|14.76|> <|14.76|> our mortality is to come face to face with the reality of how deeply and truly we need<|22.48|> <|22.48|> each other."<|25.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This sentiment, of course, was not present in the rich man's consciousness when, while<|7.72|> <|7.72|> scheming to enrich himself all the more, his life suddenly ended in isolation of his own<|14.98|> <|14.98|> making.<|16.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Another casualty of the self-made person, a term at odds with communities that show up on one another's doorsteps with food and drink and a listening ear.<|13.76|> <|15.22|> None of us are self-made.<|18.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We are most fully human when we realize our utter dependence on one another, the earth<|7.38|> <|7.38|> and the God who accompanies us even in the valley of the shadow of death.<|13.46|> <|14.74|> Whether that valley be the seeming desolate landscape of mass gun violence, or<|21.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> or the isolating forces of lethal white supremacy,<|4.32|> <|4.74|> or the terror of waking up with Walmart on your to-do list<|10.16|> <|10.16|> only to arrive and realize it is now a crime scene.<|15.68|> <|16.82|> It is the God revealed in Jesus Christ<|19.26|> <|19.26|> who emboldens us in those moments<|22.02|> <|22.02|> to push through the impulse to give up,<|26.08|> <|26.62|> to retreat,<|27.78|> <|28.34|> to,<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> it is only in that thwarting of isolation and embracing of the humane dimensions of our common<|7.00|> <|7.00|> life that wisdom is found. And when death comes, however unexpected, we are then joyful and ready.<|18.44|> <|20.28|> Amen.<|21.08|>
